macro_segments.title: The Base Ketubah Amount and the Principle of Tenai Beis Din

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah: 200 for a besulah, 100 for an almanah
Rabbi Meir's Position: Anything Less Than Standard is Be'ilas Zenus
The Two Interpretations of Be'ilas Zenus: Censure vs. Voiding
Rabbi Yehuda's Position: Starting with Standard Amount and Writing Receipts
The Gemara's Attribution of the Mishnah to Rabbi Meir

macro_segments.title: Limitations on Limiting Liens: The Seifa and the Debate over Achrayus

micro_segments.title: The Seifa: Writing Liens on Only One Field Still Creates Full Liens
Rabbi Yehuda's Principle: Achrayus Ta'us Sofer—Liens Inherent in Documents
Rabbi Meir's Opposing View: No Liens Without Explicit Writing
The Baraita on Found Loan Documents: Evidence for Rabbi Meir

macro_segments.title: Attribution of the Mishnah: Resolving the Contradiction Between Reisha and Seifa

micro_segments.title: First Resolution Attempt: The Whole Mishnah is Rabbi Meir with Distinctions

macro_segments.title: The Five Things Collectable Only from Unencumbered Property

micro_segments.title: Introduction: The Principle of Mechurar vs. Meshuabad Property
First Two Items: Peiros and Shevach Peiros in Stolen Property
Third Item: Obligation to Support Stepchildren (Mezonot)
Fourth and Fifth Items: Documents Without Written Liens
Conclusion: These Five Items Have No Liens on Sold Property

macro_segments.title: Attribution of the Mishnah: Resolving the Contradiction Between Reisha and Seifa (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Apparent Contradiction: Reisha Sounds Like Rabbi Meir, Seifa Like Rabbi Yehuda
Second Resolution: The Mishnah is Rabbi Yehuda with Base Amount Requirement
Third Resolution: Alternative Reading of the Seifa for Rabbi Meir
The Conclusion: Most Likely the Mishnah Follows Rabbi Yehuda

macro_segments.title: Captive Women and the Issue of Ones vs. Ratzon in Rape

micro_segments.title: Avuhu deShmuel: Wife of Yisroel Taken Captive is Forbidden to Her Husband
The Concern: Beginning with Force, Ending with Willingness
Rav's Challenge: The Ketubah Language Allows Return of Captive Wife
The Distinction: Actual Rape vs. Captivity with Unknown Rape

macro_segments.title: Rava's Position: Ones at the Beginning Defines All Subsequent Willingness

micro_segments.title: Rava's Rule: Techilata Be'ones Vesofa Beratzon
Even Explicit Willingness is Overridden by the Original Ones
The Baraita Supporting Rava: Five Categories of Women

macro_segments.title: Alternative Scenarios: Women Without Clear Ones or With Different Taints

micro_segments.title: Woman Not Raped But Can Return to Husband: Techilata Be'ones
Wife of a Kohen: Even If Raped, Forbidden to Husband
Alternative Teaching: Different Categories for Different Scenarios

macro_segments.title: Captives of Bandits vs. Captives of Government: Willingness and Fear

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda: Women Taken by Bandits Can Return to Husbands
The Rabbis' Objection: They Act Willingly, Providing Food and Arrows
Response: All Actions Are Motivated by Fear, Not True Willingness
The Line: Once Released and They Stay, It Becomes True Ratzon
Parallel to Rambam: Threat vs. Opportunity to Escape

macro_segments.title: Government vs. Bandit Captivity: Context-Dependent Status

micro_segments.title: Braita: Captives of Government Considered Raped, Bandits Are Not
Resolution: Distinction Between Achashverosh and Ben Nezar
Ben Nezar as Both King and Bandit: Relative Status Matters
Humorous Aside: Comparing to a Nouveau Riche Yacht Owner

macro_segments.title: Ketubah Obligations and the Wife of a Kohen: Redemption and Divorce

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Ketubah Language for Wife of Yisroel vs. Wife of Kohen
Abaye: Almanah to Kohen Gadol Still Has Redemption Obligation
The Logic: Redemption is Not an Ongoing Marital Obligation
Counterexample: Mamzeres to Yisroel Has No Redemption Obligation

macro_segments.title: Rava's Refinement: Redemption Only Applies When Captivity is the Cause

micro_segments.title: Rava's Principle: The Obligation Must Address the Specific Impediment
Application: Kohen and Almanah vs. Other Forbidden Marriages

macro_segments.title: The Debate on Redemption Obligations for Divorced or Forbidden Couples

micro_segments.title: Scenario: Husband Makes Vow That Requires Divorce, Then Wife Captured
Rabbi Eliezer: Must Redeem Then Divorce and Pay Ketubah
Rabbi Yehoshua: Pay Ketubah Without Redemption Obligation
Rabbi Natan's Question: Does Timing of Vow vs. Captivity Matter?
Sumchos's Answer: Vow Must Precede Captivity for No Redemption
The Concern: Avoiding Husband's Scheme to Escape Redemption

macro_segments.title: Attribution of the Debate to Kohen vs. Non-Kohen Cases

micro_segments.title: The Gemara Links the Tanna Debate to Kohen Wife Cases
Abaye Follows Rabbi Eliezer: Redemption Still Applies
Rava Follows Rabbi Yehoshua: Only Ketubah Payment, No Redemption
Conclusion: The Debate Applies Specifically to Forced-Divorce Cases
